text.title: confidence-thresholded-cascade Failure Modes
text.summary: Five failure modes with observable symptom, root cause, and repair.

section.title: Failure modes

antipattern.description: Threshold set by intuition ("0.5 feels reasonable") rather than from eval data.
antipattern.reason: Accuracy at that threshold is unknown; production quality drops silently because the cascade ships answers from confidence buckets where the cheap model is wrong half the time.

section.p: Symptom: eval-set accuracy drops 5-15 pp vs strong-only baseline; users complain about specific bad answers. Root cause: no calibration step. Fix: run the calibration script on 100+ tasks, plot accuracy per confidence bucket, set threshold at the bucket where accuracy crosses the SLA target.

antipattern.description: Applying the cascade to a task where confidence is meaningless (creative writing, planning).
antipattern.reason: The cheap model emits high confidence on low-quality output because there is no objective signal to ground self-rating. Cascade makes quality worse than single-strong.

section.p: Symptom: low escalation rate (<5%) but eval quality drops vs strong-only. Root cause: task does not match cascade applicability. Fix: skip cascade; use strong model directly, or split the task into a classification subtask (cascade) plus a generation subtask (strong only).

antipattern.description: Always escalating regardless of confidence, often introduced "to be safe" after a complaint.
antipattern.reason: The cascade has reverted to single-strong with extra latency. All the cost savings disappear and the cheap leg becomes pure overhead.

section.p: Symptom: escalation rate close to 100%; cost matches strong-only baseline. Root cause: defensive code path. Fix: remove the unconditional escalate; if quality concerns remain, raise the threshold instead.

antipattern.description: Cascade with 4+ levels (e.g. Haiku → Sonnet → Opus → external eval API).
antipattern.reason: Each level adds latency and routing complexity; the marginal savings beyond level 3 fall below the operational cost of the extra hop.

section.p: Symptom: p99 latency exceeds SLA; routing logic has more code than the cascade primitives. Root cause: depth crept up incrementally. Fix: cap at 3 levels; merge fine-grained intermediate stages.

antipattern.description: Cheap and strong models from the same family with the same training data (Haiku and Opus, both Claude).
antipattern.reason: The cheap model makes the same systematic mistakes as the strong model in many cases, so escalation does not recover quality on those tasks. Cross-family pairs often outperform.

section.p: Symptom: escalated answers still wrong on the tasks where cheap was confidently wrong. Root cause: correlated errors. Fix: test a cross-family cheap model (Gemini Flash, Llama 3.3) and pick the pair with the largest accuracy lift on escalated cases.
